text: The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to open the lesser sac, preserving the gastrovibrioic arcade while dividing the short gastric vessels. Care is taken to gently handle the uppermost short gastric vessels to prevent bleeding. The posterior gastric artery, originating from the splenic artery, is divided to separate the stomach from the upper pole of the spleen. The lesser sac is opened to the right, exposing the pancreas beyond the tumor for further dissection. The stomach is retracted by hitching it to the anterior abdominal wall, providing clear access to the surgical field.

The surgeon begins the pancreatic dissection by identifying the splenic artery at the upper border of the pancreas. The peritoneum is opened, and fat is dissected away to reveal the artery, which is identified by its pulsations. The artery is carefully controlled and slung to prevent injury to the surrounding vasa vasorum and avoid bleeding. Gentle traction is applied to ensure a substantial length of the artery is dissected free, and it is controlled using styes or hemologs.

The surgeon identifies and divides a polar branch to the spleen and a small pancreatic branch arising from the splenic artery. The remaining peritoneum at the upper border of the pancreas is divided, avoiding injury to the left gastric vessels. The splenic flexure of the colon is taken down to improve access to the distal body and tail of the pancreas. The peritoneum on the lower border of the pancreas is opened to access the retropancreatic planes.

The surgeon develops the avascular retropancreatic plane using blunt dissection and occasional harmonic device, lifting the pancreas and embedded splenic vein anteriorly. Posteriorly, the kidney, renal vein, and aorta are visible, bound by Gerota's fascia. The splenic vein is carefully dissected, slung, clipped, and divided to prevent bleeding, especially in cases with large tumors or spleens. The pancreas is then separated from the Gerota's fascia by dissecting the loose areolar tissue laterally towards the lienorenal ligament.

The surgeon performs pancreatic transection before dividing the lino renal ligament, ensuring the pancreas is stable at both ends. A plane is created across the full thickness of the pancreas, excluding vessels, from the lower to the upper border. An umbilical tape is used to lift the pancreas, facilitating dissection and widening of the retropancreatic plane for the introduction of the endo-GIA stapler. Sharp and blunt dissection creates a window large enough for the stapler, avoiding proximal vascular stumps in the staple line. A small feeder branch from the left colic vessels is divided, and the lower part of the pancreas and tumor are separated from the mesocolon until reaching the lino renal ligament, which is lifted and divided.

The surgeon leaves some fat on the spleen to facilitate holding and lifting it without causing splenic tears, allowing for clear visibility during transection. Upward traction on the pancreatic tissue lifts the pancreas-spleen complex, enabling easy division of the lino renal ligament.

A white pancreatic tunnel is developed using blunt and sharp dissection, allowing the introduction of the stapler. The surgeon frees the upper border of the tumor and the upper pole of the spleen from the retroperitoneum and the left crus, completing the mobilization. The pancreatic transection is performed using an endo-GIA stapler with a gold reload due to the thickness of the pancreas. Compression is maintained for three minutes to ensure hemostasis. A small sliver of pancreatic tissue not transected by the stapler is clipped and divided, and the remaining retroperitoneal tissue tethering the tumor is divided to complete the resection.

The resected specimen is placed into a specimen bag and pushed down into the pelvis for retrieval through a small incision. Retroperitoneal lavage is performed, and hemostasis is secured. The surgeon applies Nectacryl glue to the pancreatic staple line to aid hemostasis, and a drain is placed in the left upper quadrant.
